title: Seafood production: wild fish catch vs. aquaculture
subtitle: Aquaculture is the farming of aquatic organisms including fish, molluscs, crustaceans and aquatic plants. Capture fishery production is the volume of wild fish catches landed for all commercial, industrial, recreational and subsistence purposes.
caption:
Seafood production: wild fish catch vs. aquaculture

Aquaculture is the farming of aquatic organisms including fish, molluscs, crustaceans and aquatic plants. Capture fishery production is the volume of wild fish catches landed for all commercial, industrial, recreational and subsistence purposes.

This stacked area chart displays global seafood production trends from 1960 to 2022, measured in million tonnes. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 250 million tonnes, while the horizontal axis marks decades from 1960 through 2022. The chart distinguishes between two categories: capture fisheries, represented by the lower reddish-brown area, and aquaculture production, shown as the upper blue area. In 1960, total production sits below 50 million tonnes, with capture fisheries comprising the vast majority of the volume. Over the following decades, the capture fisheries band rises gradually, reaching approximately 90 to 100 million tonnes by the early 1990s. From the mid-1990s onward, the capture fisheries volume stabilizes, fluctuating within a narrow band between roughly 90 and 100 million tonnes through 2022. In contrast, the aquaculture band remains minimal until the 1980s, after which it expands steadily. By 2010, the blue aquaculture area surpasses the height of the red capture fisheries area, indicating that farmed production volume exceeded wild catch volume. By 2022, the total height of the stacked areas reaches just above 200 million tonnes, with the aquaculture component accounting for more than half of the total global production volume.
